front: Concept: 'entities should not be multiplied without necessity' - the principle of parsimony named for the author of Summa Logicae
Concept: Quine's 'Plato's beard' paradox ('nonbeing must in some sense be') illustrates how nonexistent objects 'dull' this principle
Concept: Walter Chatton created an 'anti' form of this principle and frequently feuded with its formulator
Philosopher: transitioned from a 'fictum theory' to an 'intellectio theory' of universals, arguing that universals are acts of intellection rather than real entities
work: Nominalism and the Problem of Universals

front: Philosopher: reduced Aristotle's ten categories to substance and quality, using 'Socrates is similar to Plato' to show seven categories were unnecessary
Work: develops a theory of mental language using 'natural signs' and subordinates written and spoken language to thought; also outlines supposition theory (personal, simple, material)
Philosopher: distinguished between intuitive cognition (delivers knowledge of an object's existence) and abstractive cognition (existence has no bearing on the thought)
Philosopher: David Chalmers's only history-of-philosophy paper addresses this thinker's theory of 'simple connotative terms' and 'synonymy'
Philosopher: this Franciscan friar's idealization of poverty fueled a dispute with Pope John XXII, whom he called a heretic for rejecting Apostolic Poverty
Philosopher: argued that no property rights existed in the Garden of Eden; the foremost representative of the via moderna
